reason: Both are factually correct on the core claim (exactly-once delivery is impossible; at-least-once + idempotency is standard). A is concise and correct but stops at the general principle. B explicitly names the delivery-vs-processing distinction the teammate is likely confusing, explains the underlying reason (can't distinguish a slow ack from a lost one), and gives two concrete implementation mechanisms (dedup keys, transactional/outbox patterns) plus a useful caveat that even 'exactly-once' systems like Kafka EOS only guarantee this within their own boundary and external side effects still need separate handling. This extra content is substantive and decision-relevant, not padding, and directly helps resolve the teammate's misconception. B is more useful without sacrificing correctness.
